title: Cosmic Mystery: Is This a Superkilonova?
summary:
Imagine two dead stars, each about the size of San Francisco but weighing more than the Sun, spiraling into each other and crashing. That's a kilonova—a rare explosion that creates gold and uranium. Scientists have only confirmed one, back in 2017. Now they might have found a second, but it's wrapped in a mystery.

On August 18, 2025, LIGO (the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-wave Observatory) in the US and Virgo in Italy detected a gravitational wave signal. Within minutes, an alert went out to astronomers worldwide. The signal suggested two merging objects, with at least one unusually small. "This quickly got our attention as a potentially very intriguing event candidate," says David Reitze, executive director of LIGO.

A few hours later, the Zwicky Transient Facility at Palomar Observatory spotted a fading red source in the same patch of sky, about 1.3 billion light-years away. Named AT2025ulz, it initially looked just like the 2017 kilonova—red and fading fast. But then it changed: it brightened again, turned blue, and showed hydrogen in its spectrum. That's typical of a supernova, not a kilonova.

Most astronomers would have moved on, but Mansi Kasliwal's team at Caltech didn't. "At first, for about three days, the eruption looked just like the first kilonova in 2017," she says. "Everybody was intensely trying to observe it, but then it started to look more like a supernova, and some astronomers lost interest. Not us."

Their theory? This could be a "superkilonova"—a kilonova triggered by a supernova. Imagine a massive star exploding and leaving behind two tiny neutron stars that then collide. The supernova debris hides the kilonova, making it look like a single, weird event. Co-author Brian Metzger of Columbia University says this is possible if the neutron stars spiral inward and merge inside the expanding supernova.

If confirmed, AT2025ulz would be the first observation of this predicted phenomenon. The study is published in The Astrophysical Journal Letters. For now, it's a cosmic puzzle that keeps astronomers guessing.
why_it_matters: If confirmed, this event could reshape our understanding of how heavy elements like gold form in the universe and reveal that extremely violent collisions produce more complex explosions than previously thought.

keywords.explanation: A huge explosion that happens when two dead stars crash into each other, creating heavy elements like gold.
Ripples in space created by powerful cosmic events, like star collisions.
A tiny, super-dense leftover of a star after it explodes; a single teaspoon would weigh billions of tons.
A rare type of explosion in which a kilonova happens inside a supernova, making it look like one strange event.
The range of colors (light) from an object; scientists study it to learn what the object is made of.
The Laser Interferometer Gravitational-wave Observatory, a huge instrument in the US that detects gravitational waves.

questions.question: What did LIGO and Virgo detect on August 18, 2025?
What was the initial name given to the object spotted by the Zwicky Transient Facility?
Why did some astronomers lose interest in AT2025ulz?
options: It started to look like a supernova
questions.correct_answer: It started to look like a supernova

questions.question: What is the theory of a 'superkilonova'?
options: A supernova that creates a black hole
A kilonova triggered by a supernova explosion
Two neutron stars merging without any debris
A star turning into a neutron star
questions.correct_answer: A kilonova triggered by a supernova explosion

questions.question: Who led the Caltech team that continued studying the object?
How far away is the event AT2025ulz?

background_read: Kilonovas are extremely rare; only one event, GW170817, has been confirmed, back in 2017, using both gravitational waves and light.
LIGO (the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-wave Observatory) is a pair of massive detectors in the United States that can sense tiny ripples in space caused by violent cosmic events.
Neutron stars are the collapsed cores of massive stars that have exploded; they are among the densest objects in the universe, with a mass greater than the Sun packed into a city-sized sphere.
The Zwicky Transient Facility is a telescope at Palomar Observatory that automatically scans the sky for sudden changes, like new stars or galaxy activity.
Article_Structure: LEAD: A possible second kilonova discovery – but with a twist
KEY EVENT: LIGO/Virgo detect a gravitational wave signal on Aug 18, 2025
├─ Signal indicates two merging objects, one unusually small
FOLLOW-UP: Zwicky Transient Facility finds red, fading source AT2025ulz
└─ Then it brightened, turned blue, showed hydrogen (supernova-like)
RESOLVER: Most astronomers lost interest, but Mansi Kasliwal's team persisted
THEORY: 'Superkilonova' – two neutron stars merge inside an expanding supernova debris cloud
└─ Debris hides the kilonova, making it look like one weird event
OPEN QUESTION: If confirmed, this would be the first observation of a predicted phenomenon; paper published in The Astrophysical Journal Letters

perspectives.description: Astronomers who study kilonovas gain a new kind of cosmic explosion to analyze, potentially expanding knowledge of neutron star mergers and element formation.
Skeptics argue the evidence is circumstantial; the object could simply be a rare supernova without a hidden kilonova. More data or a second similar event is needed for confirmation.
The case highlights the power of combining gravitational-wave detectors with traditional telescopes, and shows how unexpected data can lead to new theoretical models.
Future upgrades to LIGO and new observatories like the Einstein Telescope may spot more such events, either confirming the 'superkilonova' class or revealing even stranger phenomena.
